sourceTitle: How to set up your Notion workspace for your team

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of organizing the sidebar in a Notion workspace?

options.text: To make the workspace look more colorful
options.explanation: Appearance alone isn’t the goal; the sidebar is about accessibility and findability of information.

options.text: To keep information centralized and easy to locate for all team members
options.explanation: A clean, well‑structured sidebar ensures that project pages and important docs are quickly accessible.

options.text: To limit the number of pages a team can create
options.explanation: Sidebar organization doesn’t restrict page creation; it simply categorizes existing pages.

options.text: To prevent guests from viewing any content
options.explanation: Guest access is controlled by permissions, not by how the sidebar is organized.

questions.question: Which Notion feature allows an admin to prevent teammates from editing top‑level pages in the sidebar?

options.text: Teamspace Settings → Security toggle
options.explanation: The Security toggle in Teamspace Settings lets admins lock sidebar editing, but only on Enterprise plans.

options.text: Workspace Settings → Appearance
options.explanation: Appearance settings affect visual themes, not edit permissions.

options.text: Member roles → Viewer
options.explanation: Roles control overall workspace capabilities, not specific sidebar edit rights.

options.text: Database permissions → Read‑only
options.explanation: Database permissions apply to individual databases, not the sidebar structure.

questions.question: When inviting a new teammate, which role can invite additional members but cannot change workspace settings?

options.text: Member
options.explanation: Members cannot invite new people; they have the most limited permissions.

options.text: Membership admin
options.explanation: Membership admins can invite others and manage groups, but they cannot alter workspace settings.

options.text: Workspace owner
options.explanation: Owners can change workspace settings, which exceeds the described limitation.

options.text: Guest
options.explanation: Guests have no rights to invite members; they only have access to specific pages.

questions.question: Why does the guide recommend using a single large database for shared documentation?

options.text: It reduces the total number of pages you have to create.
options.explanation: While fewer pages may result, the main advantage is about flexible filtering, views, and transparency.

options.text: It allows properties, filters, and views to be applied universally, fostering transparency and customization.
options.explanation: One big database lets teams slice information in many ways while keeping everything in one place.

options.text: It prevents any teammate from editing any content.
options.explanation: Permissions are still controllable; a single database does not lock editing.

options.text: It automatically syncs with external project management tools.
options.explanation: Syncing with external tools isn’t an inherent feature of a single database.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a suggested method for encouraging collaboration in Notion?

options.text: Using @ mentions to tag teammates
options.explanation: Mentions are a core collaboration tool recommended in the guide.

options.text: Assigning a page owner for updates
options.explanation: Page owners help keep content current, as the guide advises.

options.text: Setting up automated email reminders via external services
options.explanation: The guide mentions Notion’s built‑in reminders, but not external automated email setups.

options.text: Leaving block comments for granular feedback
options.explanation: Block comments are highlighted as a way to give detailed feedback.

questions.question: What advantage do database templates provide as a team scales?

options.text: They lock the database structure so no changes can be made.
options.explanation: Templates don’t lock structures; they provide a starting point that can still be edited.

options.text: They ensure consistent processes and can be updated with new learnings.
options.explanation: Templates promote consistency and can be refined as the team discovers better ways to work.

options.text: They automatically assign tasks to the correct person.
options.explanation: Assignment still requires manual or automation setup; templates just pre‑populate fields.

options.text: They prevent duplicate entries in the database.
options.explanation: Duplication control is managed by filters or unique properties, not by templates.

questions.question: Which property type would be best for tracking the stage of a project (e.g., Planning, Development, Review)?

options.text: Person
options.explanation: Person assigns a user, not a status label.

options.text: Select
options.explanation: Select lets you choose a single value from a predefined list, perfect for stages.

options.text: Date
options.explanation: Date records a point in time, not a categorical stage.

options.text: Number
options.explanation: Number is for numeric values, not descriptive stages.

questions.question: If you need to view all launch dates from a roadmap database in a calendar format, which Notion feature should you use?

options.text: Table view
options.explanation: Table view shows rows and columns, not a calendar layout.

options.text: Board view
options.explanation: Board view organizes items by status columns, not by dates.

options.text: Calendar view
options.explanation: Calendar view displays entries based on a date property, ideal for launch dates.

options.text: List view
options.explanation: List view shows items in a simple vertical list, not a calendar.

questions.question: What limitation applies to the sidebar‑locking feature in Notion?

options.text: It can only be enabled by workspace owners on any plan.
options.explanation: Only Enterprise plan admins can toggle the lock, not all owners on any plan.

options.text: It is available on the Enterprise plan and requires admin permissions.
options.explanation: The guide specifies that sidebar locking is an Enterprise‑only feature and only admins can activate it.

options.text: It disables all editing of pages for all members.
options.explanation: Locking only prevents editing of the sidebar structure, not page content.

options.text: It automatically grants guest access to all top‑level pages.
options.explanation: Guest access is managed separately via permissions, not by sidebar locking.

questions.question: Which of the following actions helps a team quickly add many members without sending individual invites?

options.text: Enable "Allowed email domains" for automatic joining
options.explanation: Allowing a company’s email domain lets anyone with that domain join automatically, streamlining bulk onboarding.

options.text: Create a new Teamspace for each new member
options.explanation: Teamspaces organize content, not onboarding speed.

options.text: Assign every new member the Workspace owner role
options.explanation: Owner role gives extensive permissions and isn’t a method for bulk invites.

options.text: Use database templates to add members
options.explanation: Database templates are for content creation, not for adding users.
